title: Single-/Few-Human–AI Firms and Single-/Few-Human–AI–Robot Firms
subtitle: New Archetypes under the MMC Framework
title_full: Single-/Few-Human–AI Firms and Single-/Few-Human–AI–Robot Firms: New Archetypes under the MMC Framework
title_normalized: single-/few-human-ai firms and single-/few-human-ai-robot firms: new archetypes under the mmc framework

description: This working paper introduces Single-/Few-Human–AI Firms (S/F-HAI-F) and Single-/Few-Human–AI–Robot Firms (S/F-HAIR-F) as distinct micro- and mini-scale firm archetypes under the MMC framework. It examines their production functions, capital structures, platform dependencies, sectoral boundaries, risk profiles, evolutionary paths, and implications for firm theory and public policy.
abstract: Advances in generative AI, multi-agent systems, and automation are enabling a new class of firms in which one or a few humans, supported by AI and sometimes robots, can perform at scales once requiring much larger organizations. This paper introduces two such archetypes within a micro- and mini-scale firm framework (MMC): the Single-/Few-Human–AI Firm and the Single-/Few-Human–AI–Robot Firm. Although legally classified as firms, these entities differ fundamentally from traditional organizations in their production functions, capital structures, and risk profiles. These firms are not merely automated sole proprietorships, but organizational forms in which algorithmic capital functionally substitutes for internal labor hierarchies. S/F-HAI-F is characterized by AI-intensive, cognition-focused production, extreme labor leverage, high fixed and near-zero marginal costs, and strong dependence on digital platforms. S/F-HAIR-F extends this model by integrating robotic capital, allowing single- or few-human firms to directly engage in manufacturing, agriculture, logistics, and other physical sectors through micro-scale, automated operations. The paper compares these two archetypes and argues that they represent distinct, path-dependent trajectories rather than stages of a single evolution. The analysis shows that existing firm theories remain applicable but must be extended to account for algorithmic and robotic capital, platform governance, and minimal human headcount. The paper concludes by outlining key implications for firm theory and public policy, including taxation, regulation, and workforce transformation in an economy increasingly shaped by human–AI–robot micro-firms.

series_information.series_description: Working paper in the F-series folder of the EPINOVA Research repository, focused on foundational and theoretical work related to AI-mediated economic and organizational transformation.

framework.analytical_focus: Single-/few-human micro-firm archetypes enabled by AI, multi-agent systems, automation, and robotic capital

core_archetypes.name: Single-/Few-Human–AI Firm
core_archetypes.definition: A production unit consisting of one or a very small number of human decision-makers plus AI systems, producing primarily informational, knowledge-based, or decision-oriented services.
core_features: High fixed costs and near-zero marginal costs within capacity limits
Structural dependence on AI, cloud, data, and platform providers
sectoral_embedding: Media, content creation, branding, and personal IP operations

core_archetypes.name: Single-/Few-Human–AI–Robot Firm
core_archetypes.definition: A single-/few-human firm that integrates AI systems with a non-negligible stock of robotic capital to directly produce tangible goods or physical services.
core_features: Robotic capital and automated physical execution
Medium-asset structure with maintenance, depreciation, and obsolescence risks
Exposure to safety, equipment, environmental, product-liability, and local regulatory risks
sectoral_embedding: Inspection robots, drones, autonomous vehicles, and automated production cells

main_concepts: Firm archetypes under minimal human headcount
Algorithmic capital as a substitute for internal labor hierarchies
Robotic capital as a pathway from cognitive services to physical production
Platform dependence and quasi-hierarchical platform governance
Path-dependent differentiation between S/F-HAI-F and S/F-HAIR-F

main_formulas.name: Human headcount constraint
main_formulas.description: Defines the single-/few-human condition, where H denotes the number of human principals and H̄ is a small upper bound such as 1–3.

main_formulas.name: S/F-HAI-F production function
main_formulas.description: Represents output as a function of human labor input, AI-related capital, and conventional capital.

main_formulas.name: S/F-HAIR-F production function
main_formulas.description: Extends the S/F-HAI-F production function by incorporating robotic capital K_r for physical-sector production.

dimensions.name: Human labor input
dimensions.description: Human time, skills, expertise, decision-making capacity, and social capital supplied by one or a very small number of human principals.

dimensions.name: AI-related capital
dimensions.description: Model subscriptions, fine-tuned weights, vector databases, AI agents, automation workflows, and data pipelines.

dimensions.name: Robotic capital
dimensions.description: Robots and automated physical systems such as collaborative robot arms, drones, 3D printers, CNC modules, inspection robots, and autonomous vehicles.

dimensions.name: Conventional capital
dimensions.description: Computers, software, office space, basic tools, and other non-AI/non-robotic assets.

dimensions.name: Human headcount
dimensions.description: The number of human principals involved in decision-making and day-to-day operation, constrained to a very small number.

key_findings: One or a few humans supported by AI can increasingly perform productive functions that previously required larger organizations.
S/F-HAI-F and S/F-HAIR-F remain legally firms, but their internal production functions, capital structures, and risk profiles differ substantially from conventional firms and sole proprietorships.
S/F-HAI-F relies on algorithmic capital to substitute for internal labor hierarchies and is strongest in digital, cognitive, and knowledge-intensive sectors.
S/F-HAIR-F extends the model into physical sectors by adding robotic capital, enabling micro-scale manufacturing, agriculture, logistics, and urban-service operations under a very small human core.
The two archetypes are related but path-dependent trajectories rather than stages of a single linear evolution.
Existing firm theories remain applicable but require extension to account for algorithmic capital, robotic capital, platform governance, and extreme labor leverage.
Policy implications include taxation, social protection, platform and infrastructure regulation, sector-specific safety regulation, education, and workforce reskilling.

typological_comparison.sf_hair_f: Tangible goods or physical services integrated with cognitive decision-making

typological_comparison.sf_hai_f: Human labor, AI capital, conventional digital tools
typological_comparison.sf_hair_f: Human labor, AI capital, robotic capital, conventional capital

typological_comparison.sf_hai_f: Platform dependence, data governance, AI policy and cloud-service risk
typological_comparison.sf_hair_f: Platform and data risk plus safety, equipment, environmental, and product-liability risk
Infrastructure-constrained by power, connectivity, transport, industrial space, and local regulation

typological_comparison.sf_hai_f: Deepening cognitive specialization and scaling intangible outputs
typological_comparison.sf_hair_f: Densifying and coordinating physical capacity while maintaining a small human core

policy_implications: Tax systems and social safety nets may need adaptation for highly leveraged micro-firms with minimal human headcount.
Platform and infrastructure regulation should address structural dependence on a small number of AI and cloud providers.
Sector-specific regulation is required when micro-scale robotic producers enter manufacturing, agriculture, logistics, and physical services.
Education and reskilling systems should prepare workers to design, supervise, and govern S/F-HAI-F and S/F-HAIR-F systems.
